title: Delta-Neutral: Gamma vs. Theta

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-06-finance.md

setup.narrative: You are LONG a European call AND short Delta = N(d_1) shares of the underlying. The portfolio is delta-neutral. The stock then moves immediately (up OR down).
rules: Delta-neutral: first-order P&L from any small dS is zero.
GAMMA = d^2 V / dS^2 > 0 for a long call -- the portfolio benefits from any move (positive convexity).
setup.question: Is the position an arbitrage? What balances the gamma benefit?

learningObjectives: Compute the second-order P&L for a delta-neutral portfolio: ~ (1/2) * Gamma * (dS)^2.
Recognize that the portfolio LOSES VALUE OVER TIME (negative theta) if the stock doesn't move.
Understand the BSM PDE Theta + (1/2) * sigma^2 * S^2 * Gamma = r * V as the gamma-theta balance.

stages.label: Second-order P&L
stages.prompt: Long call + short Delta shares. P&L from small dS = ?

options.text: P&L ≈ Delta * dS - Delta * dS + (1/2) * Gamma * (dS)^2 = (1/2) * Gamma * (dS)^2 > 0 for any dS != 0.

stages.explanation: First-order terms cancel by delta-hedging. Second order: option gains (1/2)*Gamma*(dS)^2; stock has no second-order term. Net: (1/2)*Gamma*(dS)^2 > 0 -- the position WINS on any move (up or down).

stages.label: Why isn't this arbitrage?
stages.prompt: If gamma always pays off, why isn't this a free lunch?

options.text: Theta is NEGATIVE: the position LOSES money as time passes if the stock doesn't move.  Theta decay offsets gamma profits exactly when realized vol equals implied vol.

stages.explanation: Theta decay (Theta = dV/dt) is the cost of holding a long-option position. Each day that S doesn't move, you lose -Theta * dt > 0 (positive money out). The benefit comes only IF the stock moves enough to compensate.

stages.label: BSM PDE balance
stages.prompt: Recall the BSM PDE for V: dV/dt + r*S*dV/dS + (1/2)*sigma^2*S^2*d^2V/dS^2 = r*V. For a delta-neutral portfolio Pi = V - Delta*S, we have dPi/dS = 0. The BSM PDE specializes to:

options.text: Theta + (1/2)*sigma^2*S^2*Gamma = r*V.  Gamma profit (per unit time) equals Theta decay (per unit time) PLUS interest on V -- exactly balanced under BSM.

stages.explanation: Under risk-neutral pricing, Gamma profit (1/2)*sigma^2*S^2*Gamma is the EXPECTED gain from S moves, exactly canceling -Theta + r*V. So a delta-neutral hedger breaks even in expectation under BSM.

stages.label: Commit and trader interpretation
stages.prompt: What's the practical implication for a delta-hedged long-option trader?

options.text: P&L is positive iff REALIZED volatility > IMPLIED (used to set Theta).  This is the 'long gamma, short theta' play -- profitable when realized > implied vol, loss otherwise.

stages.explanation: Long gamma = bet on big moves; short theta = pay rent each day. Net P&L = realized variance - implied variance (per unit gamma). Delta-hedged option positions are essentially BETS ON VOLATILITY, not direction.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: Verify the BSM relation Theta + (1/2)*sigma^2*S^2*Gamma = r*V on a specific example (e.g., ATM call with sigma=0.2, T=1, r=0).
If you SHORT the call and long Delta shares, you have NEGATIVE gamma. What's the bet?
Connect to variance swaps -- formal contracts on (realized vol)^2 - (implied vol)^2.
